node_2: justifications rooted in cognitive sciences and causality
edge: Deep learning algorithms are being justified with references from cognitive sciences and causality.
The challenge of extracting meaningful symbols from low-level observations like images is still unresolved.
The problem of extracting meaningful symbols from low-level observations is sometimes referred to as disentanglement or causal representation learning.
The question of identifiability in representation learning has been the focus of recent efforts.
Representation learning and nonlinear independent component analysis (ICA) are related topics with respect to identifiability.
Object-centric representation learning aims to encode information about different objects separately in the representation.
The reason why Object-centric representation learning can perform segmentation without any supervision is poorly understood.
Additive decoders allow to generate novel images never seen during training via Cartesian-product extrapolation.
Purple regions correspond to latents/observations seen during training.
The blue regions correspond to the Cartesian-product extension.
In this example, the learner never saw both balls high, but these can be generated nevertheless thanks to the additive nature of the scene.
Essentially, a decoder f (z) acting on a latent vector z ∈ Rdz to produce an observation B∈B f (B)(zB) where B is a partition of {1, . . . , dz}.
f (B)(zB) are “block-specific” decoders and the zB are non-overlapping subvectors of z.
This class of decoder is particularly well suited for images x that can be expressed as a sum of images corresponding to different objects.
Unsurprisingly, this class of decoder bears similarity with the decoding architectures used in OCRL (Section 2).
Our identifiability results provide conditions under which exactly solving the reconstruction problem with an additive decoder.

The conditions provided in the context explain how solving the reconstruction problem with an additive decoder helps identify the latent blocks zB, which in turn validate the identification of latent variables as per the context.

The additive decoders can generate images never seen during training by recombining observed factors of variations in novel ways, leading to the term 'Cartesian-product extrapolation'. The latent blocks zB play a role in this process as mentioned in the context.

The ability of additive decoders to generate images through recombining observed factors of variations, termed as 'Cartesian-product extrapolation', is connected to the identification of latent variables. This connection is highlighted in the context.

The problem of identifiability is that, given only the distribution over x, it is impossible to distinguish between the two models (f , z) and ( ˆf , ˆz).
The problem of identifiability is that, given only the distribution over x, it is impossible to distinguish between the two models (f , z) and ( ˆf , ˆz).
This is problematic when one wants to discover interpretable factors of variations since z and ˆz could be drastically different.
There are essentially two strategies to go around this problem: (i) restricting the hypothesis class of decoders ˆf and/or (ii) restricting/adding structure to the distribution of ˆz.

By doing so, the hope is that the only bijective mappings v keeping ˆf and ˆz into their respective hypothesis classes will be trivial indeterminacies such as permutations and element-wise rescalings.
Our contribution, which is to restrict the decoder function ˆf to be additive (Definition 1), falls into the first category.

Other restricted function classes for f proposed in the literature include post-nonlinear mixtures, local isometries, conformal and orthogonal maps as well as various restrictions on the sparsity of f.
Methods that do not restrict the decoder must instead restrict/structure the distribution of the latent factors by assuming, e.g., sparse temporal dependencies, conditionally independent latent.

The concept of sparse temporal dependencies is related to the concept of conditionally independent latent variables, as they both involve temporal dependencies and latent variables with certain conditions.
The interventions targeting the latent factors are related to the support of the latents, as both concepts involve observations and manipulation of latent factors.

The study suggests that identifiability analysis of decoders can lead to a better understanding of creativity in generative models, paving the way for exploration in function classes like masked decoders used in OCRL.
